stem: The support agent receives an ambiguous refund request it cannot confidently resolve within policy. What is the most appropriate orchestration decision?
options: Guess a resolution to preserve the first-contact-resolution metric
Call escalate_to_human with the gathered context when confidence or policy coverage is insufficient
Loop indefinitely calling lookup_order until certain
Refuse to respond
rationale: A well-designed agent knows its limits: when confidence or policy coverage is insufficient it should escalate to a human with the context it has gathered, rather than guess (harming trust) or loop.

stem: Which is the best design for the process_refund MCP tool given it moves money?
options: Make it fire-and-forget with no confirmation
Give it a precise input schema, require explicit parameters (order id, amount), and design it to be idempotent/confirmable so retries don't double-refund
Let the model pass free-form text and parse it server-side
Combine refund and escalation into one ambiguous tool
rationale: Money-moving tools need a precise input_schema, explicit required parameters, and idempotency so retries or duplicate tool calls do not cause double refunds. Free-form input and merged responsibilities invite errors.

stem: To keep the agent reliable across a long multi-turn dispute, which context-management practice helps most?
options: Send the entire raw chat and all tool outputs every turn with no structure
Maintain concise, structured state (verified facts, order status, decisions) so key context persists without unbounded growth
Clear all context each turn to save tokens
Rely on the model to remember across separate API calls with no history
rationale: Reliability comes from managing context deliberately: carry concise structured state forward rather than dumping everything (which risks context bloat and distraction) or discarding needed context.

stem: For a large, risky refactor across many files, which Claude Code approach is most appropriate before making changes?
options: Direct execution immediately without review
Use plan mode to produce and review a plan before Claude edits files
Delete the files and regenerate from scratch
Disable CLAUDE.md so there are no constraints
rationale: Plan mode lets Claude Code propose a plan you review before it edits, which is appropriate for large/risky changes. Direct execution suits small, low-risk tasks.

stem: Which belong in a well-designed CLAUDE.md for the team's repository? (Select TWO.)
options: Project conventions, build/test commands, and architectural notes
Reusable guidance Claude should apply consistently across the repo
Secrets such as API keys and tokens
A copy of the entire dependency source tree
rationale: CLAUDE.md should capture conventions, commands, and durable guidance Claude applies consistently. Secrets must never be committed, and vendoring all dependency source into it is inappropriate.

stem: During a long Claude Code session the context is filling with irrelevant history, degrading focus. What is the best reliability practice?
options: Keep appending everything indefinitely
Curate context — clear or summarize stale content and provide only the files/scope relevant to the current task
Randomly truncate the oldest half of the context
Increase temperature to compensate
rationale: Curating context (clearing/summarizing stale content, scoping to relevant files) preserves reliability. Indefinite growth and blind truncation both harm results.

stem: In the coordinator/subagent research system, what is the coordinator's primary architectural responsibility?
options: Doing all searching, analysis, and writing itself
Decomposing the task, delegating to specialized subagents, and synthesizing their results
Storing the final report in a database
Replacing the need for any subagents
rationale: In an orchestrator/worker pattern the coordinator decomposes the task, delegates to specialized subagents, and integrates their outputs — the core of multi-agent orchestration.

stem: A search subagent fails on some queries. What should it return so the coordinator can proceed reliably?
options: A generic "error" string with no detail
Structured error context: failure type, what was attempted, any partial results, and alternative approaches
Nothing, and stop the whole run
A hallucinated result to avoid reporting failure
rationale: Returning structured error context (failure type, attempt, partial results, alternatives) lets the coordinator proceed and annotate coverage gaps. Generic errors, silent failure, or fabrication all reduce reliability.

stem: The document-analysis subagent exposes tools to the coordinator. Which tool-design choice best supports composition?
options: One giant tool that does search, analysis, and formatting together
Focused tools with clear names, precise input schemas, and structured outputs the coordinator can consume
Tools that return unstructured prose only
Tools with no descriptions to keep the schema small
rationale: Composable multi-agent systems rely on focused, well-described tools with precise schemas and structured outputs. Overloaded or undocumented tools are hard for the model to use correctly.

stem: To help engineers explore an unfamiliar codebase, which built-in Claude Code tools are most directly useful?
options: Read, Grep, and Glob to locate and read relevant files
Only Bash to run the app
A web browser tool only
None; the model should guess file contents
rationale: Read, Grep, and Glob let the agent locate and read the right code, which is the foundation of codebase exploration. Guessing or relying solely on running the app is unreliable.

stem: The productivity agent must reach an internal ticketing system. What is the appropriate integration mechanism?
options: Hardcode credentials into the prompt
Expose the ticketing system through an MCP server providing well-scoped tools
Ask the user to paste ticket data every time
Fine-tune the model on the ticket database
rationale: An MCP server is the standard way to connect an agent to an external system through well-scoped tools/resources. Hardcoding secrets or manual pasting are poor designs, and fine-tuning is unnecessary here.

stem: For automating a repetitive multi-step task, which agent design is most robust?
options: A single prompt that must get everything right at once
An agent loop that plans, calls tools, checks results, and iterates until the task's success criteria are met
Running all steps blindly in parallel with no verification
Hardcoding outputs for every possible input
rationale: A verifying agent loop (plan → act → check → iterate against success criteria) is robust to variation. Single-shot prompts and unverified parallelism are brittle.

stem: The CI code-review bot produces too many false-positive comments. Which prompt-engineering change best reduces them?
options: Tell it to comment on everything it notices
Give explicit criteria for what warrants a comment, ask it to report only high-confidence, actionable issues, and provide few-shot examples of good vs noise
Raise temperature so it varies its output
Remove all instructions and let it decide
rationale: Defining clear criteria, requiring high-confidence actionable findings, and providing few-shot examples of signal vs noise directly reduces false positives. Broad instructions or higher temperature increase noise.
referenceText: Prompt engineering — be clear and use examples

stem: How is Claude Code best invoked inside a non-interactive CI/CD pipeline?
options: It cannot run in CI
In headless/non-interactive mode driven by a command and prompt, with output consumed by the pipeline
Only by a human typing into a terminal
By committing prompts to the repo and waiting
rationale: Claude Code supports headless/non-interactive execution suitable for CI, where it runs from a command/prompt and the pipeline consumes its output. It is not limited to interactive terminals.

stem: To make generated test cases trustworthy, the prompt should ask Claude to:
options: Generate as many tests as possible regardless of relevance
Ground tests in the actual code/spec, cover meaningful cases and edge cases, and avoid duplicating existing tests
Only test the happy path
Assert that all tests pass without running them
rationale: Trustworthy test generation is grounded in the real code/spec, targets meaningful and edge cases, and avoids duplicates — not sheer volume or happy-path-only coverage.

stem: To guarantee extracted records match a required JSON structure, the most reliable design is to:
options: Ask for JSON in prose and regex-parse the reply
Define a tool with a JSON input_schema and use tool use so outputs conform to the schema, then validate against it
Lower max_tokens until only JSON fits
Trust the model to always emit valid JSON
rationale: Structured output is most reliable when the schema is enforced via tool use and then validated. Prose-plus-regex and truncation are brittle, and blind trust fails on edge cases.

stem: Some source documents are messy and occasionally missing fields. Which approach best maintains accuracy and downstream reliability?
options: Force the model to invent values for missing fields
Have the model output field-level confidence and mark missing/uncertain fields for review rather than fabricating
Reject any document that isn't perfect
Ignore validation to keep throughput high
rationale: Emitting field-level confidence and flagging missing/uncertain values for review preserves accuracy and enables safe downstream use. Fabricating values or skipping validation harms reliability.

stem: Which prompt-engineering technique most improves extraction consistency on ambiguous formats?
options: Zero instructions
A few targeted few-shot examples that demonstrate the desired parsing and output format
Higher temperature
A longer max_tokens only
rationale: Few-shot examples that demonstrate the exact parsing and output format are a documented, effective way to improve consistency on ambiguous inputs.
